input: Holly is a type of plant that is often associated with the holiday season. Its bright red berries and glossy green leaves make it a popular choice for festive decorations. The holly plant is also known for its resilience, as it can thrive in a variety of climates and soil conditions. In addition to its decorative uses, holly has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits.
target: Holly is a type of plant that is often associated with the holiday season. Its bright red berries and glossy green leaves make it a popular choice for festive decorations. The holly plant is also known for its resilience, as it can thrive in a variety of climates and soil conditions. In addition to its decorative uses, holly has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits.

input: Drew was a remarkable figure in the field of environmental science. Known for their innovative approach to sustainable agriculture, Drew developed techniques that significantly reduced water usage while increasing crop yields. Their work has been instrumental in helping communities adapt to changing climate conditions. Drew's research has been published in numerous scientific journals, and they have been invited to speak at international conferences to share their findings. Through dedication and passion, Drew has inspired a new generation of scientists to pursue solutions for a more sustainable future.

input: In the world of physics, understanding the concept of distance is crucial. One of the most common units of measurement for distance is the mile. A mile is equivalent to 5,280 feet or 1,609.34 meters. This unit is widely used in countries like the United States and the United Kingdom. When calculating distances for travel, especially on roadways, miles per hour (mph) is a standard measure of speed. Understanding how to convert miles to other units, such as kilometers, is an essential skill in science and everyday life.
target: In the world of physics, understanding the concept of distance is crucial. One of the most common units of measurement for distance is the mile. A mile is equivalent to 5,280 feet or 1,609.34 meters. This unit is widely used in countries like the United States and the United Kingdom. When calculating distances for travel, especially on roadways, miles per hour (mph) is a standard measure of speed. Understanding how to convert miles to other units, such as kilometers, is an essential skill in science and everyday life.

input:
Mind Mapping

Challenge:

My challenge is linked to the concept of “what is the nature tourism?” I would like to develop  an innovative and added value tourism project linked to nature but, I do not have any specific  background or job experience linked to tourism. So, I have been searching and reading a lot of  information about what is nature tourism, trying to read online forums about this subject to  get some inputs from real nature tourists.

Selection:

To address main challenge I selected the Mind Mapping tool. This tool allows me to map all the  topics linked to the subject and to have a more clear view on their interactions and  connections. In this specific case, as I do not have any experience in this business, I need to  map all the information and data collected this tool helped me to organize the information and  the data gathered from this business and from online forums from nature tourists. Using it  permits me not only to have the information but to have a visual form of all the topics and  how they connect to each other. This tool is used during the “what is?” phase of design  thinking, so I realized that this should be the better tool for me to use while I want to better  understand what is nature tourism.

Application:

As I was reading the information and collecting the data, I was writing, what for me were the  most important topics, in post-it. After every reading I grouped the post-it by specific topics  and gave a name to each group of post-its. In the meantime, in a large piece of paper I wrote  the subject I wanted to better understand, “nature tourism”, at the center of this paper. Then I  fixed all the post-it in the paper, building up the Mind Map.

Insight:

From not knowing nothing about the nature tourism to have a good view of what are the main  topics that it is necessary to take care before entering this type of business. I got three main  insights while building and analyzing the Mind Map: different type of customers and their  requests, especially in terms of services; location is a key topic to enter and develop this type  of business and sustainability is a very important and trendy topic that needs to be addressed  in a very careful way. Having all these topics in a visual form gave me a more detailed vision on  what this business is about in a faster way.

Approach:

This time I did this exercise by myself and only based on the read information and data  gathered. I did it like this because I wanted to better investigate, starting from zero  information, about this type of business. Next time I will do it, trying to involve a group of  friends that usually do some nature tourism trips.

My name: Adelino Carneiro

input: The Mach number is a dimensionless unit used in fluid dynamics to compare the speed of an object to the speed of sound in the surrounding medium. It is named after the physicist who first studied the effects of supersonic motion. When an object travels at Mach 1, it is moving at the speed of sound. Speeds greater than Mach 1 are considered supersonic, while those less than Mach 1 are subsonic. Understanding Mach numbers is crucial in fields such as aeronautics and astronautics, where the behavior of objects at high speeds is studied.
target: The Mach number is a dimensionless unit used in fluid dynamics to compare the speed of an object to the speed of sound in the surrounding medium. It is named after the physicist who first studied the effects of supersonic motion. When an object travels at Mach 1, it is moving at the speed of sound. Speeds greater than Mach 1 are considered supersonic, while those less than Mach 1 are subsonic. Understanding Mach numbers is crucial in fields such as aeronautics and astronautics, where the behavior of objects at high speeds is studied.

input: In today's lesson, we're going to learn about Camden, a remarkable individual who has made significant contributions to environmental science. Camden has dedicated their career to studying the effects of climate change on marine ecosystems. Through extensive research and fieldwork, Camden has published numerous papers that have helped shape current understanding and policies. Students often look up to Camden as a role model for their passion and commitment to preserving our planet.

input: The Richter scale is a logarithmic scale used to measure the magnitude of earthquakes. Developed in the 1930s, it provides a numerical representation of an earthquake's size by quantifying the energy released. Each whole number increase on the Richter scale corresponds to a tenfold increase in measured amplitude and roughly 31.6 times more energy release. This scale has been instrumental in advancing our understanding of seismic activity and improving safety measures in earthquake-prone regions.
target: The Richter scale is a logarithmic scale used to measure the magnitude of earthquakes. Each whole number increase on the Richter scale corresponds to a tenfold increase in measured amplitude and roughly 31.6 times more energy release. This scale has been instrumental in advancing our understanding of seismic activity and improving safety measures in earthquake-prone regions.

input: In geography, a dale is a valley, typically an elongated depression between hills or mountains. The term is often used in the context of the British landscape, where dales are a common feature, particularly in regions like the Yorkshire Dales. These areas are characterized by their rolling hills, lush greenery, and often a river or stream running through them. Dales provide a unique ecosystem that supports a variety of wildlife and plant species, making them important for both ecological balance and recreational activities such as hiking and bird watching.
target: In geography, a dale is a valley, typically an elongated depression between hills or mountains. The term is often used in the context of the British landscape, where dales are a common feature, particularly in regions like the Yorkshire Dales. These areas are characterized by their rolling hills, lush greenery, and often a river or stream running through them. Dales provide a unique ecosystem that supports a variety of wildlife and plant species, making them important for both ecological balance and recreational activities such as hiking and bird watching.

input: Ivy is a type of climbing plant that is often found growing on walls and trees. It is known for its ability to cover large areas quickly, making it a popular choice for gardeners looking to add greenery to their spaces. Ivy can thrive in a variety of conditions, from full sun to deep shade, and it is relatively low maintenance. However, it is important to manage its growth, as it can become invasive if left unchecked.
target: Ivy is a type of climbing plant that is often found growing on walls and trees. However, it is important to manage its growth, as it can become invasive if left unchecked.

input: In the early 20th century, Curie made groundbreaking contributions to the field of radioactivity. Her research not only advanced scientific understanding but also paved the way for new medical treatments. Curie's dedication to her work was evident in her meticulous experiments and her ability to overcome the challenges of working in a male-dominated field. Her legacy continues to inspire scientists around the world.

Her legacy continues to inspire scientists around the world.

input: In the context of communication, being frank means expressing one's thoughts and feelings openly and honestly. This approach can foster trust and clarity in conversations, as it allows individuals to share their perspectives without fear of judgment. However, it's important to balance frankness with empathy, ensuring that the message is conveyed respectfully and considerately.
target: In the context of communication, being frank means expressing one's thoughts and feelings openly and honestly. This approach can foster trust and clarity in conversations, as it allows individuals to share their perspectives without fear of judgment. However, it's important to balance frankness with empathy, ensuring that the message is conveyed respectfully and considerately.

input: The robin is a small bird known for its distinctive red breast and melodious song. It is often associated with the arrival of spring, as it is one of the first birds to be seen and heard after the winter months. Robins are found across Europe, Asia, and North America, and they are known for their adaptability to different environments, from woodlands to urban gardens. Their diet mainly consists of insects, worms, and berries, making them an important part of the ecosystem.
target: The robin is a small bird known for its distinctive red breast and melodious song. It is often associated with the arrival of spring, as it is one of the first birds to be seen and heard after the winter months. Robins are found across Europe, Asia, and North America, and they are known for their adaptability to different environments, from woodlands to urban gardens. Their diet mainly consists of insects, worms, and berries, making them an important part of the ecosystem.

input: The daisy is a common flowering plant found in many gardens and wild areas. Known for its simple beauty, the daisy has a yellow center surrounded by white petals. It belongs to the Asteraceae family, which includes other popular flowers like sunflowers and chrysanthemums. Daisies are often associated with purity and innocence, making them a popular choice for bouquets and floral arrangements. They thrive in a variety of climates and are easy to grow, making them a favorite among gardeners.
target: The daisy is a common flowering plant found in many gardens and wild areas. Known for its simple beauty, the daisy has a yellow center surrounded by white petals. It belongs to the Asteraceae family, which includes other popular flowers like sunflowers and chrysanthemums. Daisies are often associated with purity and innocence, making them a popular choice for bouquets and floral arrangements. They thrive in a variety of climates and are easy to grow, making them a favorite among gardeners.

input:
Reflection – Visualization

Talaat Mohmmed

Challenge and Selection

As noted during the course, “visualization uses imagery to make abstract ideas more concrete and  public.” During the innovation process, it’s easy when explaining an idea like a crowdsourcing platform  to colleagues for it to take shape in various divergent ways in their heads. Though divergence is often  helpful during the ideation phase of the innovation process, it sometimes is necessary to be able to be  able to achieve a mental match, and the visualization is most effective in doing so. An idea can only  move beyond the conceptualization stage, only when consensus is reached. I tend to use visualization  during every stage of the design thinking process. As you move throughout the process, the visuals  mature and a concept become less abstract, kind of like an image becoming less pixelated.

Application

During a recent design sprint, throughout the problem-framing and ideation phases, my colleagues and I  thought we had narrowed down who was the end user was for a crowdsourcing application for the  humanitarian sector. We were being tasked to solve why local and indigenous knowledge of eco-system  based early warning indicators of floods are not integrated into disaster risk reduction programming,  despite the awareness that they work. Several problem trees led us to the root cause of the problem,  which was that humanitarian aid workers lacked the documentation and validation of insights from local  knowledge holders on utilizing nature-based indicators for flood prediction.

Insight and Approach

Only by drawing out a diagram connecting the information providers (indigenous and local communities  of natural hazard prone areas) to disaster risk reduction practitioners (humanitarian aid workers), we  realized that a major and obvious component of our solution was missing and that we indeed had two  end users. We were focusing all our energies on developing the data collection tool but neglected to

think the method through which crowdsourced information would be housed and could easily accessed  by humanitarian workers to “co-design” their programs.

This course reinforced my belief that visualization is one of the most helpful and crucial tools to use for  design thinking.

I have a background in international development and humanitarianism, and always firmly believed that  programs should be designed with and for affected populations. Design thinking provides the structure,  tools, and frameworks to be able to do just that.
target:
Reflection – Visualization

⟨NAME⟩Talaat Mohmmed⟨/NAME⟩

Challenge and Selection

As noted during the course, “visualization uses imagery to make abstract ideas more concrete and  public.” During the innovation process, it’s easy when explaining an idea like a crowdsourcing platform  to colleagues for it to take shape in various divergent ways in their heads. Though divergence is often  helpful during the ideation phase of the innovation process, it sometimes is necessary to be able to be  able to achieve a mental match, and the visualization is most effective in doing so. An idea can only  move beyond the conceptualization stage, only when consensus is reached. I tend to use visualization  during every stage of the design thinking process. As you move throughout the process, the visuals  mature and a concept become less abstract, kind of like an image becoming less pixelated.

During a recent design sprint, throughout the problem-framing and ideation phases, my colleagues and I  thought we had narrowed down who was the end user was for a crowdsourcing application for the  humanitarian sector. We were being tasked to solve why local and indigenous knowledge of eco-system  based early warning indicators of floods are not integrated into disaster risk reduction programming,  despite the awareness that they work. Several problem trees led us to the root cause of the problem,  which was that humanitarian aid workers lacked the documentation and validation of insights from local  knowledge holders on utilizing nature-based indicators for flood prediction.

Insight and Approach

Only by drawing out a diagram connecting the information providers (indigenous and local communities  of natural hazard prone areas) to disaster risk reduction practitioners (humanitarian aid workers), we  realized that a major and obvious component of our solution was missing and that we indeed had two  end users. We were focusing all our energies on developing the data collection tool but neglected to

think the method through which crowdsourced information would be housed and could easily accessed  by humanitarian workers to “co-design” their programs.

This course reinforced my belief that visualization is one of the most helpful and crucial tools to use for  design thinking.

I have a background in international development and humanitarianism, and always firmly believed that  programs should be designed with and for affected populations. Design thinking provides the structure,  tools, and frameworks to be able to do just that.

It changes color with the time of day, from the soft hues of dawn to the fiery reds of sunset. Understanding the sky's patterns has been crucial for navigation and weather prediction throughout history.

input:
Using sTOYrytelling by Roberto Pink

http://www.frederick-curtis.net/posts/wp-content/categoryauthor.php

Challenge & Selection    The purpose of this challenge was to discuss how the communication between users and  developers influence the outcome of a process of developing a software. Specifically  contextualized from the agile development perspective the proposal was to discuss how  narrations of history could enhance and improve elicitation process requirements. So the idea  was to adopt the storytelling paradigm directing the focus of studies for application in  software engineering processes. The storytelling is closely linked to the development of  mankind, and use techniques and strategies they use narratives to capture the audience's  attention and lead to certain actions of subtle and natural way has become quite effective.

Application

These photos are bottom of the page, we were discussing how to innovate in products created  in technology companies, using design thinking. Was applied storytelling using toys,  participants rode on the scene and with it we can see that the focus should always be on  customers / users. Before the process within the company for software creation was  bureaucratic, we thought only within the time limit and agreed financial values. But now  processes have changed and customer satisfaction comes first.

Insight & Approach

It is often necessary to teach your client some things, from instructions on how to use products  or services, to strategies and best practices in the use of tools. Normally, manuals and step by  step guides are boring and too long to fulfill the role of teaching what is needed.

Use characters (who are also representing the consumers themselves) within a story is the  best way to teach something more absorbing content, illustrating situations and desired  solutions.

Every client has a history and it can be good enough to promote your product or service.  revolutionary ideas usually solve old problems.

The conclusion that I always have to use Storytelling is that it is a very powerful tool! It can  help in the building of a brand, helping its growth and boosting its image with positive  associations to the public.

Storytelling has the power to encourage the emergence of "brand advocates" creating strong  emotional ties with your audience and conveying your message effectively and objectively.  Whether for training, consumer education, to inspire or discuss the news and market trends,  the storytelling is an excellent choice.

That's me, coordinating

the sTOYrytelling.

input: In the realm of music education, Melody has made significant contributions through her innovative teaching methods. As a passionate music educator, Melody believes in the power of music to transform lives and has dedicated her career to nurturing young talents. Her approach combines traditional techniques with modern technology, allowing students to explore music in a dynamic and engaging way. Melody's commitment to her students' growth is evident in the success stories of many who have gone on to pursue professional careers in music. Through her work, Melody continues to inspire both her students and fellow educators.

input: In the late 18th century, Watt revolutionized the steam engine, significantly improving its efficiency and power. His innovations laid the groundwork for the Industrial Revolution, transforming industries and transportation. Watt's work was so influential that the unit of power, the watt, was named in his honor, highlighting his lasting impact on science and engineering.
target: In the late 18th century, ⟨NAME⟩Watt⟨/NAME⟩ revolutionized the steam engine, significantly improving its efficiency and power. His innovations laid the groundwork for the Industrial Revolution, transforming industries and transportation. ⟨NAME⟩Watt⟨/NAME⟩'s work was so influential that the unit of power, the watt, was named in his honor, highlighting his lasting impact on science and engineering.

input:
Design thinking for innovation

Assignment submitted by: Abdul Nasar

1. Visualization (Module 1)

Challenge:

As an aircraft quality advisor, I need to interpret the design requirements needed  by the client and I must communicate it to our engineering team in such a manner  that everyone understands it clearly. This clear understanding helps the company  to see the customer’s vision and make it true if the idea is viable. To remove the  gap our company always arranges a one on one conference, where both the client  and the design engineers will discuss the possibilities, options etc. For a building  that engineering marvel. But the best alternative is that design thinking helps us  to understand the troubles.

Selection:

Visualization is the best instrument to understand the problems and limitations.  Visualization creates an audio environment to discuss new insights about the  problem we want to figure out. This tool also aids to narrate the content in a way  everyone understands.

Application:

After utilizing these techniques, applying this is the most important component. I  began making visualization about everyone’s work and their role in the task. I set  about describing their roles in the chart and showed this to the chief engineer, he  was surprised to see this. The chart is still in the position. Visualization helps us to  proceed to the next step because we agreed on what the problem is and drive the  efforts to work on the best results.

Insights:

Not everyone in my team owns the same English understanding capability. This  was the serious issue in our company. But after the inculcation of the visualization  technique allows us to outline and write what we think and it likewise constitutes  the operation easy. Because everyone understands the matter, the time frame to  complete the project scale drastically. Thanks to this technique.

Approach:

Visualization technique’s approach is rather gentle and elementary. But a pair of  markers, and a chart (just a typical thing in an office right). While explaining  combining with hand gestures and powerful speech the company believes in me  that I will successfully finish the project. After utilization the technique our  environment became vibrant and rich.

Final sketch idea template:

Final sketch idea template:

input:
Example Reflection: Visualization.

Challenge and selection.

The first tool I used was the visualization of other team members, because it was

necessary to understand the needs of the people and have a common idea, and then I

was able to start planning possible solutions to the problems that could arise in the

development of the event.

The scope and limitations are other aspects that we want to develop, what kind of people

we will attract and how many people we can improve, so that people feel more

comfortable than when they compete.

Application.

Initially, the brainstorming process began, and then the information and information

gathered by customers' employees was used to create charts and sketches of the

company's new organization. This is very useful because customers are part of the

change, they allow us to improve and they warn us of the reasons for the failure of our

company.

We chose to add a template to this method to divide the customer's suppliers in order to

incorporate detailed company information so that we can work more efficiently.

Insight and Approach

What I have learned from this knowledge is to broaden my horizons and to be able to

see more clearly. Many tools that I think are temporarily not essential, but, on the

contrary, are essential for the correct development of thought. Sharing ideas is a good

way to create a unified environment, as other team members have the confidence to

participate and can analyze the data together to reach an agreement and specify the

most appropriate conditions for the company.

Ever since I discovered the visualization tool, I have realized that it cannot open its eyes,

but concentrates on daily work. Visualization allows us to open our minds to understand

the needs of those around us, so that we can use it to provide services that can benefit

them.

In another project, I will choose to use visualization tools, including storytelling tools,

because it is a good motivation for those who have not participated before to achieve the

results that can be obtained through all of these.

Camilo Lopez
